title: Leptogramma pilosa
taxonomy: Leptogramma pilosa is classified within the family of ferns, specifically in the genus Leptogramma, and more broadly within the division Pteridophyta, which includes all vascular plants that produce spores. It belongs to the class Polypodiopsida and further classified into the order Polypodiales. Within the order, it is classified into the family Polypodiaceae. This classification reflects the plant's characteristics, such as its fern-like appearance and spore-producing reproductive structures. Further research may be necessary to determine the exact classification within the family Polypodiaceae.

intro: Leptogramma pilosa, also known as Khas or Indian Sarsaparilla, is a perennial plant species native to the tropical regions of India and Southeast Asia. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which can aid in the treatment of various skin conditions such as eczema and acne. The bioactive constituents of Leptogramma pilosa include flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the plant is commonly prepared as a decoction or a tincture, and is also used to make a paste or powder for topical application.
